text:
> These days I think most people expect "click in scrollbar gutter" to jump the scroll thumb to that spot, but the other option was "scroll in that direction", which could make a comeback here.
There's also been a 3rd option that the vertical position of the click affects how far you jump with each click.

Emacs did this <https://github.com/emacs-mirror/emacs/blob/f8a447f60a44c1c02275345f6060cc98370077ad/doc/emacs/frames.texi#L867-L877|~20 years ago>:
>   The left and right buttons in the scroll bar scroll by controlled increments.  kbd{Mouse-1} (normally, the left button) moves the line at
> the level where you click up to the top of the window.  kbd{Mouse-3} (normally, the right button) moves the line at the top of the window
> down to the level where you click.  By clicking repeatedly in the same place, you can scroll by the same distance over and over.
... before it courted standard toolkits and switched to <https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/emacs/Scroll-Bars.html|fixed size jumps>.

xterm <https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/602897/17113|can> do this, depends on one's xrdb  bindings.

<https://codefrau.github.io/Smalltalk78/|SmallTalk> did this with single mouse button: hover on the XEROX text for scroll bar to appear, then note how hovering over left/right halfs of the bar shows down/up arrow.  Try clicking both ways near the top (scrolls a little) vs. bottom (scrolls a lot).

(1) Theoretically, this gives user more control.  In practice, not sure—grabbing and dragging engages your normal eye-hand instincts for controlled positioning.
(2) It was also always hard to discover.
(3) The difference between the click position outside the handle vs. dragging the handle was always jarring.

I think I've also seen models where _distance from handle_ affects size of jump? That's much more intuitive :+1: But by that point it's close enough to "jump to where you clicked" that people didn't bother...

elements.text: These days I think most people expect "click in scrollbar gutter" to jump the scroll thumb to that spot, but the other option was "scroll in that direction", which could make a comeback here.

There's also been a 3rd option that the vertical position of the click affects how far you jump with each click.

Emacs did this

The left and right buttons in the scroll bar scroll by controlled increments.  kbd{Mouse-1} (normally, the left button) moves the line at
the level where you click up to the top of the window.  kbd{Mouse-3} (normally, the right button) moves the line at the top of the window
down to the level where you click.  By clicking repeatedly in the same place, you can scroll by the same distance over and over.
... before it courted standard toolkits and switched to
do this, depends on one's xrdb  bindings.

did this with single mouse button: hover on the XEROX text for scroll bar to appear, then note how hovering over left/right halfs of the bar shows down/up arrow.  Try clicking both ways near the top (scrolls a little) vs. bottom (scrolls a lot).

(1) Theoretically, this gives user more control.  In practice, not sure—grabbing and dragging engages your normal eye-hand instincts for controlled positioning.
(2) It was also always hard to discover.
(3) The difference between the click position outside the handle vs. dragging the handle was always jarring.

I think I've also seen models where
affects size of jump? That's much more intuitive
But by that point it's close enough to "jump to where you clicked" that people didn't bother...
